retrieved_docs.title: Federal Medication Development Regulation -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: If drug sponsors can demonstrate adequate safety and efficacy during clinical trials phases I to III, they may subsequently submit a New Drug Application (NDA) or Biologics License Application (BLA). [9] If the FDA accepts the quality of clinical trial data, then the NDA or BLA will be approved, and drug manufacturers can begin marketing. One of many FDA-expedited review programs may shorten the developmental process. These expedited processes are granted for investigational drugs that fill an unmet medical need or represent a substantial increase in efficacy for severe medical conditions. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Aducanumab -- Indications -- FDA Conditional Approval Initially Granted in July 2021 under the Program for Accelerated Approval
retrieved_docs.content: In November 2020, the FDA's Peripheral and Central Nervous System Drugs Advisory Committee met to discuss the aducanumab BLA. A series of 4 votes culminated in the near-unanimous (there was a single abstention) verdict that the advisors did not find reasonable grounds to consider the results of the clinical trials as evidence of aducanumab's effectiveness for treating AD. Within days of the July 2021 accelerated approval decision, 3 members of the Advisory Committee, comprising one-third of its nongovernment members, resigned, citing "serious potential to impair future research" and deeming the FDA's ruling "the worst approval decision that FDA has made....." [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Federal Medication Development Regulation -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Priority review can be used for an NDA or BLA to decrease the FDA review period and may be combined with other expedited review programs. [11] Fast-track review can be requested for IND applications for drugs that fulfill an unmet medical need. Fast-track review allows drug sponsors to meet with the FDA to advance drug development. Breakthrough therapy review can be requested for drugs with clinical data demonstrating significant improvement over existing drugs. Breakthrough drugs also allow drug sponsors to meet with the FDA and sometimes enable sponsors to avoid components of the standard review process. Accelerated approval is intended for developing drugs with long-term clinical endpoints that may be difficult to measure during clinical trials. Other expedited review programs may also be used alongside accelerated approval.

retrieved_docs.title: Federal Medication Development Regulation -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: The FDA continues to monitor newly approved drugs and biologics for long-term safety. Phase IV clinical trials are used for post-market surveillance involving thousands of participants. [8] During phase IV, drug sponsors must send periodic reports on safety and tolerability to the FAERS. Additionally, the FDA regulates the use of potentially harmful drugs by requiring manufacturers to use Risk Evaluation and Mitigation Strategy (REMS) programs. Such programs were formalized by the Food and Drug Administration Amendments Act of 2007 and helped to ensure safe drug use by consumers. [12] For example, isotretinoin is commonly used for acne treatment but has teratogenic effects if taken while pregnant. Isotretinoin is dispensed by a REMS program called iPLEDGE. The iPLEDGE program mandates that female patients using isotretinoin must use 2 birth control methods, take doctor-administered pregnancy tests, and complete online educational modules to mitigate the risk of bearing children with severe congenital disabilities. [13] There are currently 61 REMS programs with varying degrees of implementation and effectiveness. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Federal Regulation of Medication Production -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: An entrant must produce evidence (under 505(d)) that the medicine is safe and efficacious when requesting clearance for an NDA. Animal and clinical (human) investigations are required as evidence. Section 505(a) prohibits the distribution of any novel medicine unless it has been FDA approved. 505(i), which permits the FDA to exempt a medication from the NDA criteria for clinical research, avoids this seemingly conflicting position. The maker must file a "Notice of Claimed Investigational Exemption for a New Drug," also known as an Investigational New Drug (IND) Application, to get this exclusion. The producer could then perform clinical tests of its IND if it is authorized. The filing of an IND follows the applicant's lengthy preclinical inquiry. The petitioner has decided that the drug has prospective worth and would be reasonably safe to try in people through laboratory experiments and animal testing. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Federal Medication Development Regulation -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: The submission of an Investigational New Drug (IND) application is required before the drug is administered to human participants. [5] Once submitted, the FDA has 30 days to decide if the drug is suitable for human testing. When the FDA approves an IND, human testing can proceed with multi-phase clinical trials, which may last 6 to 7 years and average $48 million per drug. [6] Fewer than 14% of drug candidates will reach FDA approval during this rigorous process. [7] Additionally, severe and fatal adverse events must be reported to the FDA Adverse Event Reporting System (FAERS) within 15 days. The FDA can halt clinical trials at any time if an investigational drug causes significant harm to patients. The phases of clinical trials (I to IV) are summarized in Table I. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Aducanumab -- Indications -- FDA Conditional Approval Initially Granted in July 2021 under the Program for Accelerated Approval
retrieved_docs.content: Biogen first presented positive results from its phase 1b study of aducanumab in early 2015; they enrolled the first subject in a global phase 3 clinical trial approximately 6 months later. In autumn 2019, Biogen announced plans for a regulatory filing; the Biologics License Application (BLA) was submitted to the FDA in July 2020 after delays caused by the early stages of the coronavirus pandemic.

retrieved_docs.title: Aducanumab -- Indications -- FDA Conditional Approval Initially Granted in July 2021 under the Program for Accelerated Approval
retrieved_docs.content: Eventually, Biogen completed 2 phase 3 clinical trials with identical designs. Known individually as EMERGE and ENGAGE, both trials were initially terminated on grounds of futility before they were reinstated after additional blinded data became available. Based on this additional data, the researchers determined that EMERGE had met its primary outcome measure (the CDR "sum of boxes"); ENGAGE did not meet its primary outcome, but "benefits were observed" in study participants on higher doses for more extended periods. Knopman et al analyzed the data from 2 clinical trials with aducanumab. The investigators concluded that the efficacy of aducanumab cannot be proven when the 2 clinical trials yield different results. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Aducanumab -- Indications -- FDA Conditional Approval Initially Granted in July 2021 under the Program for Accelerated Approval
retrieved_docs.content: In July 2021, the FDA granted accelerated approval for aducanumab; the decision was not based on the clinical data but rather on an intermediate outcome, namely the drug's ability to reduce the burden of excess β-amyloid in the brain and the attendant "reasonable" likelihood that amyloid reduction would benefit patients. [10] In conjunction with the approval, the FDA required that Biogen conduct a phase 4 clinical trial through 2030 to evaluate risks and benefits over the long term. Enrollment in the phase 4 trial was not a requirement for prescribing aducanumab.

retrieved_docs.title: Tapinarof -- Indications -- FDA-Approved Indications
retrieved_docs.content: In 2 identically designed phase III trials, namely PSOARING 1 and 2 trials, which were randomized, vehicle-controlled, double-blind, and multicenter, the efficacy of tapinarof cream 1% administered once daily was demonstrated. These trials spanned a duration of 12 weeks and exhibited a significant reduction in the severity of plaque psoriasis. [11] The primary endpoint for each trial was defined as a PGA (Physician Global Assessment) score response of either 0 (clear) or 1 (almost clear), in addition to a minimum decrease of 2 points at week 12 compared to the baseline. In trial 1, the results revealed a 35.4% primary endpoint response for patients in the tapinarof group, whereas the vehicle-controlled group exhibited a response of 6.0%. Similarly, trial 2 concluded with a PGA response observed in 40.2% of patients in the tapinarof group, whereas the vehicle-controlled group had a response of 6.3%. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Federal Medication Development Regulation -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Due to the stringent requirements set by the FDA, some studies have revealed the issue of “drug lag” in the US markets. Particularly in the 1970s, new drugs were often approved and marketed far earlier in European markets than in US markets. [20] This resulted from increased regulatory standards in the US, which increased the time required to verify drug safety. Therefore, while European consumers were already using novel medications, US consumers often could not access these drugs. The duration of clinical trials continues to increase across all phases as the scope of federal regulations continues to expand, which may continue to hinder the ability of the FDA to approve medications promptly. However, some evidence suggests that the burden of drug lag has decreased in recent years as federal regulations on the pharmaceutical industry have evolved. [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Glucagon-Like Peptide-1 Receptor Agonists -- Indications -- FDA-Approved Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Liraglutide/insulin-degludec has obtained FDA approval, affirming their effectiveness and safety in diabetes management. [12] [13] Because of safety concerns, research on another agent, taspoglutide, has been halted in phase III trials. [14] Recently, Orforglipron, a nonpeptide oral GLP-1 receptor agonist, has demonstrated promising results in phase 2 clinical trials. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Federal Medication Development Regulation -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: US governing authorities heavily regulate intellectual property. The US Patent and Trademark Office grants drug patents, whereas the FDA sets data exclusivity for new medicines. Exclusivity involves inhibiting the approval of competing drugs for a set period to balance novel drug creation and consumer medication accessibility. The FDA allows 5 years of exclusivity for novel small-molecule drugs and 12 years for biologics. [15] New clinical uses of existing drugs may be granted 3 years of exclusivity. Under the Best Pharmaceuticals for Children Act, 6 months of exclusivity may be added to existing pediatric drugs. Under the Orphan Drug Act, 7 years of market exclusivity is granted for treating rare diseases. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Luspatercept -- Indications -- FDA-Approved Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Luspatercept is an erythroid maturation agent approved by the FDA for treating anemia in patients with β-thalassemia who need regular red blood cell transfusions. [1] The recommended dose of luspatercept for patients with β-thalassemia is 1 mg/kg subcutaneously once every 3 weeks. [2] [3] The FDA approved this dosage based on the results of the phase III BELIEVE trial. [4] This medication promotes late-stage erythropoiesis, even in patients with β-thalassemia ineffectively controlled with erythropoiesis-stimulating agents (ESA).

retrieved_docs.title: Orphan Drug Approval Laws -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The Food and Drug Administration (FDA) qualifies a rare disease as one which affects fewer than 200,000 people or as a condition that affects greater than 200,00 persons in the United States but for which there is no reasonable expectation that pharmaceutical companies will recover the costs incurred during drug development following pharmaceutical sales. In the United States, between 6,000 and 8,000 diseases meet this condition, affecting an estimated 25 million people. [2] Despite the large number of individuals affected by rare diseases, pharmaceutical companies have been reticent to focus their efforts on drug development for these conditions. They believed that they would not feasibly recover the development costs.

retrieved_docs.title: Latanoprost -- Indications -- FDA-Approved Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Prostaglandins are most commonly used as the first line of treatment in glaucoma due to their efficacy in reducing the IOP, convenience of once-daily dosing, and acceptable safety profile. Latanoprost is the first prostaglandin analog approved by the United States Food and Drug Administration (FDA). The drug was FDA-approved for ocular use in 1996. Additionally, the fixed-dose combination (FDC) of netarsudil/latanoprost was approved by the USFDA in March 2019 for open-angle glaucoma (OAG) and ocular hypertension (OHT). [2]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 1821
retrieved_docs.content: The product “24 HR alfuzosin hydrochloride 10 MG Extended Release Oral Tablet [UroXatral]” contains alfuzosin as its active moiety, making alfuzosin the pharmacologically responsible ingredient in this extended‑release formulation. Alfuzosin is contraindicated for co‑administration with several antiretroviral agents, including lopinavir, atazanavir sulfát, fosamprenavir calcium, kobicistat, ombitasvir heminonahydrate, and paritaprevir. These contraindications indicate that patients receiving alfuzosin should not be treated simultaneously with any of these drugs, as the combination may lead to adverse drug interactions or reduced therapeutic efficacy. Recognizing and avoiding these drug‑class conflicts is essential for safe prescribing and optimal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 4 -- Community 2952
retrieved_docs.content: Angiotensin II receptor blockers (ARBs), plain constitute a pharmacologic class that includes the individual agents eprosartan, irbesartan, and olmesartan medoxomil. Each of these medications is a member of the plain ARB category and functions by selectively blocking the angiotensin II type 1 receptor, thereby inhibiting the vasoconstrictive and aldosterone‑secreting effects of angiotensin II. By reducing systemic vascular resistance, these agents are employed to manage hypertension and related cardiovascular conditions, offering therapeutic options that share a common mechanism of action while differing in pharmacokinetic profiles and dosing characteristics.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 3 -- Community 2391
retrieved_docs.content: Alfuzosin is the active moiety in the product “24 HR alfuzosin hydrochloride 10 MG Extended Release Oral Tablet [UroXatral]” and exerts its therapeutic effect through the mechanism of action classified as an adrenergic alpha‑antagonist. Alfuzosin has important drug‑interaction considerations: it is contraindicated for co‑administration with the antiretroviral agents lopinavir, dasabuvir, dasabuvir sodium monohydrate, elvitegravir, and kobicistat. Additionally, alfuzosin is contraindicated with any medication that functions as a cytochrome P450 3A4 inhibitor, because inhibition of this metabolic pathway can lead to elevated alfuzosin concentrations and increased risk of adverse effects. Clinicians should therefore avoid prescribing alfuzosin together with these specific antiviral drugs or with CYP3A4‑inhibiting agents to ensure safe and effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 2505
retrieved_docs.content: The product 24 HR alfuzosin hydrochloride 10 MG Extended Release Oral Tablet [UroXatral] contains the active moiety alfuzosin. Alfuzosin is contraindicated for co‑administration with several antiviral agents, including atazanavir sulfát, dasabuvir sodium monohydrate, fosamprenavir calcium, kobicistat, and paritaprevir dihydrate, because these drugs can interact adversely with alfuzosin. In addition, agents that act as Cytochrome P450 3A4 Inhibitors [MoA] have a contraindicated mechanism of action with alfuzosin, indicating that CYP3A4 inhibition may increase alfuzosin exposure and raise the risk of side effects. Recognizing these contraindications is essential for safe prescribing and for preventing harmful drug‑drug interactions in patients receiving alfuzosin‑containing therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2516
retrieved_docs.content: The product 24 HR alfuzosin hydrochloride 10 MG Extended Release Oral Tablet [UroXatral] contains alfuzosin as its active moiety. Alfuzosin is contraindicated for co‑administration with several antiviral agents, including Lopinavir, dasabuvir, dasabuvir sodium monohydrate, fosamprenavir calcium, kobicistat, ombitasvir heminonahydrate, and paritaprevir dihydrate. Awareness of these contraindications is essential to prevent adverse drug interactions when prescribing alfuzosin‑containing therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 1433
retrieved_docs.content: Angiotensin II receptor blockers (ARBs), plain, constitute a pharmacologic class of antihypertensive agents that act by selectively blocking the AT₁ receptor, thereby inhibiting the vasoconstrictive and aldosterone‑secreting effects of angiotensin II. Candesartan, telmisartan, and azilsartan medoxomil are each members of this plain ARB class. As members of the same therapeutic group, these three drugs share a common mechanism of action and are used to lower blood pressure, reduce cardiovascular risk, and provide renal protection in conditions such as hypertension, heart failure, and diabetic nephropathy. Although they belong to the same class, each agent has distinct pharmacokinetic properties—such as differences in half‑life, bioavailability, and receptor affinity—that can influence dosing schedules and individual patient tolerability. Recognizing candesartan, telmisartan, and azilsartan medoxomil as members of the plain ARB category helps clinicians select an appropriate agent based on specific clinical scenarios, comorbidities, and patient response, thereby optimizing cardiovascular and renal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 2597
retrieved_docs.content: The product identified as “24 HR alfuzosin hydrochloride 10 MG Extended Release Oral Tablet [UroXatral]” contains alfuzosin as its active moiety. Alfuzosin should not be co‑administered with several antiviral agents because it is contraindicated for the drug classes represented by lopinavir, darunavir, dasabuvir, kobicistat, ombitasvir, paritaprevir and paritaprevir dihydrate. These contraindications reflect clinically important drug‑drug interaction risks, and healthcare providers must avoid prescribing alfuzosin together with any of these agents to ensure patient safety and therapeutic effectiveness.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 1399
retrieved_docs.content: The product 24 HR alfuzosin hydrochloride 10 MG Extended Release Oral Tablet [UroXatral] contains alfuzosin as its active moiety. Alfuzosin is contraindicated for use with several antiretroviral agents, including atazanavir sulfát, darunavir, darunavir ethanolát, dasabuvir, ombitasvir, and ombitasvir heminonahydrate. In addition, the protease inhibitor lopinavir is also contraindicated when combined with alfuzosin. These drug‑drug interaction warnings are critical for clinicians to consider when prescribing alfuzosin to patients who are receiving any of these HIV‑targeted therapies, in order to avoid adverse effects and ensure safe patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 3 -- Community 2467
retrieved_docs.content: Angiotensin II receptor blockers (ARBs) are a class of antihypertensive medications that include the plain agents candesartan, eprosartan, irbesartan, olmesartan medoxomil, telmisartan, and valsartan. These drugs share the mechanism of blocking the angiotensin II type 1 receptor, resulting in vasodilation, reduced aldosterone secretion, and lower blood pressure. As plain formulations, they are used individually rather than in fixed‑dose combinations, allowing clinicians to select a specific ARB based on patient tolerance, pharmacokinetic profile, and therapeutic goals. Recognizing that candesartan, eprosartan, irbesartan, olmesartan medoxomil, telmisartan, and valsartan belong to the same ARB class aids prescribing decisions and optimizes management of hypertension, heart failure, and renal protection.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2601
retrieved_docs.content: AGENTS ACTING ON THE RENIN-ANGIOTENSIN SYSTEM constitute a broad pharmacologic class that includes various combination products involving angiotensin II receptor blockers (ARBs). Within this overarching group, the subclass angiotensin ii receptor blockers (arbs), combinations represents formulations that pair ARBs with other agents. A specific example of such a formulation is Angiotensin II receptor blockers (ARBs) and calcium channel blockers, which is classified as an ARB‑based combination therapy. Thus, ARB‑calcium channel blocker combinations are a distinct type of angiotensin ii receptor blockers (arbs), combinations, and all of these combination products fall under the larger category of agents acting on the renin‑angiotensin system, guiding clinicians in selecting and understanding the hierarchy of antihypertensive therapies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2179
retrieved_docs.content: N [Preparations] is a category of pharmaceutical preparations that includes both nedokromil and nusinersen. As members of this preparation class, nedokromil and nusinersen share a common formulation lineage. Nusinersen, in particular, is noted for its physiologic effect of increased protein synthesis, a mechanism that underlies its therapeutic action. Recognizing that both drugs belong to the same N [Preparations] group while nusinersen uniquely promotes protein synthesis can inform clinicians about their relatedness and distinct pharmacologic profiles.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 1653
retrieved_docs.content: Angiotensin II receptor blockers (ARBs), plain represent a class of antihypertensive medications that function by selectively inhibiting the angiotensin II type 1 receptor, thereby reducing vasoconstriction, aldosterone secretion, and sympathetic activation. Specific agents that belong to this class include olmesartan medoxomil, telmisartan, and azilsartan medoxomil. As members of the ARB family, olmesartan medoxomil, telmisartan, and azilsartan medoxomil share a common mechanism of action and are used to manage hypertension, protect renal function in diabetic patients, and treat heart failure or left‑ventricular dysfunction. Recognizing that these three drugs are individual members of the broader “angiotensin II receptor blockers (ARBs), plain” category helps clinicians select appropriate therapy based on pharmacokinetic properties, dosing convenience, and patient‑specific factors while maintaining the therapeutic benefits associated with ARB therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 4 -- Community 2931
retrieved_docs.content: The product 24 HR alfuzosin hydrochloride 10 MG Extended Release Oral Tablet [UroXatral] contains alfuzosin as its active moiety. Alfuzosin is contraindicated for co‑administration with several antiviral agents, specifically atazanavir sulfát, darunavir ethanolát, dasabuvir, ombitasvir, paritaprevir, and paritaprevir dihydrate. Recognizing these contraindications is essential for safe prescribing and for preventing adverse drug interactions when patients are receiving alfuzosin‑containing therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 2301
retrieved_docs.content: The medication 24 HR alfuzosin hydrochloride 10 mg extended‑release oral tablet (UroXatral) contains alfuzosin as its active moiety. Alfuzosin is contraindicated for use with several antiviral agents, including lopinavir, atazanavir sulfát, fosamprenavir calcium, ombitasvir heminonahydrate, paritaprevir and paritaprevir dihydrate. These contraindications indicate that co‑administration of alfuzosin with any of these drugs may lead to adverse drug interactions, and clinicians should avoid prescribing them together to ensure patient safety.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2957
retrieved_docs.content: Angiotensin II Receptor Blockers (ARBs), plain formulation, constitute a pharmacologic class used to inhibit the effects of angiotensin II on the AT1 receptor, thereby lowering blood pressure and providing cardiovascular protection. Candesartan, irbesartan, olmesartan medoxomil, and valsartan are all individual agents that belong to this ARB class. Each of these drugs shares the common mechanism of blocking angiotensin II–mediated vasoconstriction, which makes them interchangeable options for managing hypertension, heart failure, and diabetic nephropathy, while allowing clinicians to select a specific ARB based on patient tolerance, dosing convenience, and formulary considerations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 3 -- Community 2578
retrieved_docs.content: The product 24 HR alfuzosin hydrochloride 10 MG Extended Release Oral Tablet [UroXatral] contains alfuzosin as its active moiety. Alfuzosin is contraindicated for use with several antiviral and antiretroviral agents, indicating a risk of clinically significant drug‑drug interactions. Specifically, alfuzosin should not be co‑administered with the hepatitis C agents dasabuvir sodium monohydrate, fosamprenavir calcium, ombitasvir, ombitasvir heminonahydrate, and paritaprevir. In addition, the antiretroviral drug lopinavir is also listed as a contraindicated class for alfuzosin. These contraindications underscore the importance of reviewing a patient’s medication regimen for these agents before initiating alfuzosin therapy to avoid adverse effects and ensure safe patient care.
